Mortadella pizza with pistachio and grana padano

romanized: Mortadella Stracciatella Fior di Latte Pizza

food_subgroup: Pizza — topped flatbread with cheese, cured meat, and garnishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002572). No specific class exists for mortadella-and-stracciatella pizza; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a category. No specific facet code for mortadella-stracciatella topping combination; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226754000 is the generic pizza concept. No specific descendant for this topping variant; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole individual pizza of approximately 350 g, as typically served at an Italian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Pizza with meat, regular crust (FDC ID 170720) and component ingredient data for stracciatella, mortadella, pistachio, and grana padano
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists in any approved primary source for mortadella-and-stracciatella pizza. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for meat pizza (FDC ID 170720) as the base, adjusted upward for fat and protein to reflect the richer topping profile (stracciatella, mortadella, grana padano, pistachio, extra virgin olive oil). The base pizza per-100g values (energy ~266 kcal, protein ~11 g, fat ~10 g, carbohydrate ~33 g) were weighted with component ingredient data for the toppings. Final composite reflects a richer, higher-fat Italian restaurant pizza. All values are estimates and should be replaced with laboratory analysis when available.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Pizza, cheese, thin and crispy crust (GI ~50, referenced against glucose).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mortadella-and-stracciatella pizza. The closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables is cheese pizza (GI ~50, glucose reference). This pizza has a similar wheat-flour dough base and comparable fat content, which slows gastric emptying and lowers postprandial glucose response. GI 50 is used as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 94.5) / 100 = 47.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 27.0 × 3.5 = 94.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g; ~2170 mg per full serving) from mortadella, grana padano, and stracciatella
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from cured pork mortadella and aged cheeses; a single serving may exceed the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load per serving (~47 g GL) from refined wheat dough, partially offset by high fat and protein content slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this pizza in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables, as the refined wheat crust contributes a substantial glycemic load despite the moderating effect of fat and protein from the cheese and meat toppings.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~6.2 g per 100 g; ~21.7 g per serving) and high sodium from mortadella, stracciatella, and grana padano
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this pizza due to its high saturated fat and sodium content from cured pork and multiple full-fat cheeses, both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mortadella and Stracciatella Pizza is a contemporary Italian-style pizza featuring a baked wheat-flour base topped with fior di latte (fresh mozzarella), stracciatella cheese (a soft, creamy pulled-curd cheese from Puglia), slices of mortadella (a finely ground Italian cured pork sausage from Bologna, seasoned with spices and studded with fat), toasted pistachios, cherry tomatoes, grated grana padano (a hard aged Italian cheese similar to Parmigiano-Reggiano), and a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil. The pizza is baked at high temperature to achieve a crisp, lightly charred crust, and the mortadella is typically added after baking to preserve its delicate flavour and silky texture. The result is a rich, indulgent pizza with a complex flavour profile: creamy and milky from the stracciatella and fior di latte, savoury and subtly spiced from the mortadella, nutty from the toasted pistachios, and umami-rich from the grana padano. This style of pizza is associated with the modern Italian gastronomic movement and is increasingly popular in upscale Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~285 kcal per 100 g), high in fat (particularly saturated fat from the cheeses and cured meat), moderate in protein, and provides a meaningful carbohydrate load from the dough. Sodium content is high due to the mortadella and aged cheeses. It is not suitable for low-sodium or low-saturated-fat diets. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low), moderated by the high fat and protein content, but the glycemic load per full serving is substantial (~47 g) due to the large dough portion. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact match for mortadella-and-stracciatella pizza exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central meat pizza data (FDC ID 170720) adjusted for the richer topping profile. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cheese pizza, GI ~50) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but laboratory analysis of this specific dish would be required for high confidence.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this pizza category and was not estimable from component ingredients with sufficient confidence. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium uncertainty. 3. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as the pizza dough (wheat flour) is the dominant macronutrient contributor by weight. 4. This dish is served at Mingle - Funan (Singapore) at SGD 34.00. 5. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this Italian cuisine dish; the romanized Italian name 'Pizza con Mortadella e Stracciatella' is provided as the romanized_local field. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 50 × carbohydrate per serving 94.5 g) / 100 = 47.3, where serving size is 350 g and carbohydrate per 100 g is 27.0 g. 7. The input food_name 'Mortadella & Stracciatella' has been expanded to 'Mortadella and Stracciatella Pizza' to reflect the canonical dish identity as a pizza product.
